title.en: Identity Federation: SSO, SAML/OIDC & Workload Identity

description.en: Design one identity plane across Entra ID, AWS IAM, and Google Cloud IAM: human SSO via SAML/OIDC and keyless workload identity federation that eliminates long-lived credentials.

story_variants.en:
## One Person, Three Clouds, How Many Passwords?

An engineer named Asha joins your platform team on Monday. By Friday she needs access to Azure subscriptions, AWS accounts, and GCP projects. If she gets three usernames, three MFA enrollments, and three offboarding checklists, you have already lost: the day she leaves, someone will forget one of the three, and that forgotten identity is your next incident report.

The first law of multi-cloud identity: **one authoritative identity provider (IdP), everything else federates.** In Microsoft-centric enterprises that IdP is almost always **Microsoft Entra ID** (formerly Azure AD); elsewhere it may be Okta or Ping — the architecture is identical. Every cloud, every SaaS app, every CI system *trusts* the IdP rather than storing its own credentials.

## The Protocol Layer: SAML vs OIDC

- **SAML 2.0** — XML-based, browser-redirect flows, battle-tested since the mid-2000s. Still what most enterprise SSO integrations (including AWS IAM Identity Center's classic path and many legacy apps) speak.
- **OIDC (OpenID Connect)** — JSON/JWT on top of OAuth 2.0. The modern default: easier to debug, native to APIs and mobile, and — critically — the foundation of *workload* identity federation.

> **Callout:** Rule of thumb for 2025-2026: **OIDC for anything new, SAML for anything old.** You will run both for years; the strategic decision is which one your *governance tooling* treats as primary.

## Human Federation Across the Big Three

| Concern | Azure | AWS | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Identity service | Microsoft Entra ID | IAM Identity Center (on IAM) | Cloud Identity + Cloud IAM |
| Inbound federation from corporate IdP | Native (it often *is* the IdP) | SAML 2.0 / SCIM into Identity Center | SAML/OIDC into Cloud Identity; Workforce Identity Federation for direct IAM |
| Group-to-role mapping | Entra groups → Azure RBAC role assignments | IdP groups → permission sets → account roles | Google groups → IAM role bindings |
| Provisioning | SCIM / Entra provisioning | SCIM to Identity Center | Google Cloud Directory Sync / SCIM |
| Session model | Primary refresh tokens, CA policies | Temporary STS credentials per role | Short-lived OAuth tokens |

Three consequences worth internalizing:

- **Groups are the API of identity.** Nobody should ever be granted access as an individual. IdP group membership → cloud role mapping is the only pattern that survives joiner-mover-leaver reality.
- **Sessions must be short-lived everywhere.** AWS STS credentials expire in hours; Entra Conditional Access can enforce sign-in frequency; GCP tokens are short by default. Long sessions convert a phished laptop into a persistent foothold.
- **Offboarding is one operation.** Disable the IdP account; every federated session dies at next token refresh. That is the entire business case for federation in one sentence.

## Workload Identity: Killing the Secret

Humans were the easy part. The bigger 2025-era win is **workload identity federation** — machines proving who they are *without long-lived keys*:

- **GitHub Actions → AWS:** the workflow presents a GitHub-issued OIDC token; an IAM role trust policy validates issuer, repository, and branch claims; STS returns temporary credentials. No AWS access key stored in GitHub secrets.
- **GitHub Actions / external workloads → GCP:** Workload Identity Federation maps external OIDC tokens to service-account impersonation — Google explicitly discourages exported service-account JSON keys.
- **Anything → Azure:** federated credentials on an Entra app registration or managed identity accept OIDC tokens from GitHub, Kubernetes, or other issuers.
- **Inside Kubernetes:** AKS Workload Identity, EKS IRSA / Pod Identity, and GKE Workload Identity all map pod service accounts to cloud IAM identities — the same idea in three dialects.

> **Callout:** Every static credential in a CI variable, a config file, or a developer laptop is a liability with no expiry date. Leaked long-lived keys remain one of the most common cloud breach initial-access vectors. Federation replaces "secrets you must rotate" with "tokens that expire in minutes."

## Real Failure Modes

- **The break-glass paradox.** You federate everything, then the IdP has an outage and nobody can log into any cloud. Mature estates keep 2-3 monitored, vault-stored, MFA-protected *local* break-glass accounts per cloud — tested quarterly, alarmed on any use.
- **Claim-mapping sloppiness.** An OIDC trust policy that validates the issuer but not the repository claim lets *any* repo in the org (or worse, any customer of the issuer) assume your deploy role. Trust policies must pin issuer, audience, and subject claims.
- **Group explosion.** 4,000 fine-grained groups with nested membership that no human can audit. Design a naming convention and an access-review cadence before federating, not after.
- **SCIM drift.** SSO works but provisioning is manual, so people authenticate fine into accounts that no longer should exist. Federation without automated deprovisioning is half a control.

## The Architect's Deliverable

Your output for this layer is a **trust map**: which issuers each cloud trusts, which claims are validated, which groups map to which roles, and where break-glass lives. In the challenge you will build a small federation evaluator: given each application's supported protocols and an IdP capability set, decide whether it can be federated and which protocol to prefer — the same triage exercise every SSO migration program starts with.

description.en: One authoritative IdP at the center; every cloud federates inward. Break-glass accounts are the deliberate, monitored exception.

left.label: Long-lived access keys stored in CI secrets

right.label: OIDC workload federation: short-lived tokens, pinned issuer/subject claims

description.en: Static keys never expire and leak constantly. Federated tokens expire in minutes and validate exactly who is asking.
The keyless deployment flow — the same shape on AWS, Azure, and GCP.

explanation.en: The IdP capability set: modern IdPs speak both SAML and OIDC.
An app can join SSO if it shares at least one protocol with the IdP — LDAP-only legacy apps cannot.
OIDC for anything new, SAML as the fallback for anything old — the standard migration triage rule.

description.en: SSO migration triage output: what federates today, via which protocol, and what needs modernization first.

instruction.en: Build an SSO federation triage tool. IDP_SUPPORTS is the set of protocols your IdP speaks. Implement can_federate(app) -> bool (True if any of app['protocols'] is in IDP_SUPPORTS) and preferred_protocol(app) -> str ('oidc' if the app supports oidc, else 'saml' — only called for federable apps). For each app in APPS (in order) print exactly '<name>: FEDERATED via <protocol>' or '<name>: NOT_FEDERATED'. Then print 'Coverage: <federated>/<total>'. This is the first artifact of every enterprise SSO rollout.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
IDP_SUPPORTS = {"saml", "oidc"}

APPS = [
    {"name": "legacy-hr", "protocols": ["saml"]},
    {"name": "api-portal", "protocols": ["oidc"]},
    {"name": "old-erp", "protocols": ["ldap"]},
    {"name": "data-studio", "protocols": ["saml", "oidc"]},
]

def can_federate(app):
    # TODO: True if the app shares at least one protocol with IDP_SUPPORTS
    pass

def preferred_protocol(app):
    # TODO: 'oidc' if available, else 'saml'
    pass

# TODO: print '<name>: FEDERATED via <protocol>' or '<name>: NOT_FEDERATED' per app
# TODO: print 'Coverage: <federated>/<total>'
practice_challenges.expected_output:
legacy-hr: FEDERATED via saml
api-portal: FEDERATED via oidc
old-erp: NOT_FEDERATED
data-studio: FEDERATED via oidc
Coverage: 3/4

en: can_federate: use any(p in IDP_SUPPORTS for p in app['protocols']).
preferred_protocol: return 'oidc' if 'oidc' in app['protocols'] else 'saml' — OIDC always wins when both exist.
Count federated apps in the loop and finish with f'Coverage: {n}/{len(APPS)}'.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert can_federate({"name": "a", "protocols": ["saml"]}) is True
assert can_federate({"name": "b", "protocols": ["ldap"]}) is False
assert can_federate({"name": "c", "protocols": ["ldap", "oidc"]}) is True
assert preferred_protocol({"name": "d", "protocols": ["saml", "oidc"]}) == "oidc"
assert preferred_protocol({"name": "e", "protocols": ["saml"]}) == "saml"

q.en: What is the single-sentence business case for identity federation?

en: It removes the need for MFA
Disabling one IdP account kills access everywhere at next token refresh
It eliminates the need for RBAC roles

q.en: Why does workload identity federation beat storing cloud access keys in CI secrets?

en: Federated tokens are short-lived and validate pinned issuer/subject claims; static keys never expire and leak

q.en: A trust policy validates the OIDC issuer but not the subject/repository claim. What is the risk?

en: Any workload from that issuer — not just your repo — can assume the role

q.en: Why keep local break-glass accounts when everything is federated?

en: Auditors require at least five local admins
Federation is too slow for emergencies
An IdP outage would otherwise lock you out of every cloud simultaneously
Break-glass accounts are cheaper than federation
